title: Valuing a Financial: Why the Normal Toolkit Breaks
quick_definition: If you try to value a bank or insurer the way you'd value a normal company, the tools fall apart — because debt is the raw material, not a financing choice, so enterprise value and free cash flow stop meaning anything. Valuation is forced to the equity side: dividends, and price-to-book read against the returns that book earns.
explainer_covers: The two breakages, plainly (Damodaran Ch. 14): (1) you can't separate a bank's operations from its financing — deposits and debt are the product — so enterprise value (★G25) and the EV↔equity bridge (★G95) are undefined; (2) there's no meaningful capex or working capital, so free cash flow (★G92) can't be computed — regulatory capital plays the role reinvestment plays elsewhere.

What still works: value the equity directly — the dividend (plus buybacks) is the stream you can actually take out, and it's capped by the capital rules (Z5.1); and you use the cost of equity only, never a blended WACC (★G91, with the exception noted).

The sector's signature chart: price-to-book against ROE — a bank worth more than its book equity is one earning good returns on that equity; price-to-tangible-book is the honesty-adjusted version. (The comps machinery itself is ★G27/★G88's — this node adds only the bank-specific twist.)

The catch that ties it to Z3.2: a bank trading below book is either a bargain or the market's honest verdict that the loan marks are too optimistic — so the valuation question collapses back into the asset-quality question.

Duplication guard (D4), honored: DCF, multiples, and comps process are not re-taught here — Equity Research (Z3.7–3.8) and ★G88/★G90/★G27 own them; this node adds only the bend. Insurers get book/embedded-value logic with the same equity-side constraint; asset managers are the exception where the ordinary toolkit mostly works (Z2.3).

connects_to.note: NAV — the sector layer's other book-value cousin

connects_to_raw: `equity-research.z3.7`, `equity-research.z3.8` (the generic machinery — the guard), `★G25`, `★G95`, `★G92`, `★G91`, `★G27`, `★G88`, Z5.1, Z3.2, `real-estate.z5.3` (NAV — the sector layer's other book-value cousin).
real_world_layer: Damodaran, The Dark Side of Valuation, Ch. 14.

title: The Credit Lens: Sizing Up a Bank as a Borrower
quick_definition: Judging whether a bank can repay its own debt is a different craft from judging a normal company: the usual corporate credit tools don't apply, so the analysis runs on capital, asset quality, and funding stability — plus the modern wrinkle that a bank's own debt now includes instruments designed to take losses in a crisis.
explainer_covers: The Fixed Income handshake, completed: FI taught the corporate credit template and openly deferred the industry cases — this is that leg for the sector where the template breaks hardest.

The negative seam first (Part 1's callout): the corporate workhorses — EBITDA-to-interest, leverage multiples (★G74, ★G29) — are undefined for a bank. What replaces them is the classic bank checklist: capital (★G287), asset quality (★G291), management, earnings (Z3.1), and liquidity/funding — captured by the old "CAMELS" shorthand.

Funding as the bank-specific art: sticky insured deposits versus flighty wholesale money — two banks with equal capital but different funding are genuinely different credit risks (this is the run exposure, Z5.2).

The capital stack a lender faces after 2008: senior vs. subordinated vs. the newer AT1/CoCo instruments built to convert to equity or get written off when a bank gets into trouble (Ramirez Ch. 6) — "bail-in" as the deliberate replacement for taxpayer "bail-out," and why a bank's junior debt is closer to equity risk than its name suggests (★G275/★G276/★G277 for the market's read; ★G278 CDS as the hedge and the signal).
Why covenants barely feature (★G34 contrast): a bank's creditors lean on the regulator, not on loan covenants — the supervisor is the covenant package.
connects_to_raw: `fixed-income.z4.8` (attachment — the deferral completed), `★G74`/`★G29` (the negative seam), `★G287`, `★G291`, `★G275`, `★G276`, `★G277`, `★G278`, `★G34`, Z5.1, Z5.2.
